title: Hair Fall Causes & Advanced Hair Loss Treatments - Dermatologist Guide
content:
Hair Fall Causes & Advanced Hair Loss Treatments - Expert Guide by Dr. Anju Mehra

Hello, I am Dr. Anju Mehra, a dermatologist and hair specialist in Ajmer. In my clinic, I see countless patients distressed by hair thinning and hair loss. Hair fall isn't just a cosmetic concern – it deeply affects self-confidence and emotional well-being. The good news is that with today's advanced treatments, most hair loss is treatable when addressed correctly.

If you've tried various shampoos, oils, and home remedies without success, this comprehensive guide will help you understand the root causes and explore effective solutions available at our skin and hair clinic in Ajmer.

What's in this article?

  • Normal vs Excessive Hair Fall
  • Early Signs of Hair Loss
  • Main Causes of Hair Fall
  • Types of Hair Loss
  • How We Diagnose Hair Loss
  • Advanced Hair Loss Treatments
  • Home Care & Prevention Tips
  • When to See a Hair Specialist

Normal vs Excessive Hair Fall: Understanding the Difference

First, let's clear a common confusion – some hair fall is normal. Our scalp has about 100,000 hair follicles, and each hair goes through growth cycles.

  • • Normal hair fall: 50-100 hairs per day (especially during washing/combing)
  • • Excessive hair fall: More than 100-150 hairs daily, visible thinning, widening part, receding hairline
  • • Key indicator: If you notice significantly more hair on your pillow, shower drain, or hairbrush

Early Signs of Hair Loss You Shouldn't Ignore

Catching hair loss early improves treatment success dramatically. Look for these signs:

  • • Widening hair part – Can you see more scalp than before?
  • • Receding hairline – Especially at temples (male/female pattern)
  • • Excessive shedding – Clumps of hair coming out
  • • Thinning ponytail – Reduced volume when tying hair
  • • Visible scalp – Through hair, especially under light
  • • Slow growth – Hair doesn't seem to grow as before

Main Causes of Hair Fall: Understanding the Root Problem

1. Genetic Factors (Androgenetic Alopecia)

The most common cause, affecting both men and women. It's hereditary and hormone-related.

  • • Male Pattern: Receding hairline, thinning at crown
  • • Female Pattern: Diffuse thinning, widening part
  • • Mechanism: Hair follicles become sensitive to DHT (dihydrotestosterone)

2. Nutritional Deficiencies

Your hair needs specific nutrients to grow. Deficiency in any can cause hair fall. Learn about how diet affects hair health.

  • • Iron: Most common deficiency, especially in women
  • • Vitamin D: Essential for hair follicle cycling
  • • Protein: Hair is made of keratin (protein)
  • • B Vitamins: Especially Biotin (B7)

3. Hormonal Imbalances

Similar to hormonal acne, hair health is deeply connected to hormones.

  • • Thyroid disorders: Both hypo and hyperthyroidism
  • • PCOS: High androgens cause hair thinning
  • • Postpartum: Hair loss after childbirth (telogen effluvium)
  • • Menopause: Decreasing estrogen levels

4. Stress and Lifestyle Factors

Physical or emotional stress can push hair follicles into resting phase.

  • • Telogen Effluvium: Stress-induced diffuse shedding
  • • Poor sleep: Disrupts hair growth cycles
  • • Crash dieting: Rapid weight loss
  • • Smoking: Reduces blood flow to follicles

5. Medical Conditions & Medications

  • • Autoimmune: Alopecia areata (patchy hair loss)
  • • Scalp conditions: Fungal infections, psoriasis, dermatitis
  • • Medications: Chemotherapy, blood thinners, some antidepressants

6. Hair Care Mistakes

  • • Heat styling: Excessive use of straighteners, dryers
  • • Chemical treatments: Frequent coloring, perming
  • • Tight hairstyles: Ponytails, braids causing traction alopecia
  • • Wrong products: Harsh shampoos with sulfates

Different Types of Hair Loss

  • • Androgenetic Alopecia: Pattern hair loss (hereditary)
  • • Telogen Effluvium: Stress-related diffuse thinning
  • • Alopecia Areata: Autoimmune patchy hair loss
  • • Traction Alopecia: Due to tight hairstyles
  • • Scarring Alopecia: Permanent hair loss from scalp damage

How We Diagnose Hair Loss at Our Ajmer Clinic

Proper diagnosis is crucial for effective treatment. At my dermatology clinic in Ajmer, we use:

  • • Detailed History: Onset, pattern, family history, medications
  • • Trichoscopy: Scalp magnification to examine follicles
  • • Pull Test: Gently tug hair to assess shedding
  • • Blood Tests: Check thyroid, iron, vitamin D, hormones
  • • Scalp Biopsy: In rare cases for definitive diagnosis

Advanced Hair Loss Treatments Available in Ajmer

1. Topical Treatments

  • • Minoxidil: FDA-approved, stimulates hair growth, available as 2-5% solution
  • • Growth Factor Serums: Peptide-based treatments
  • • Topical Finasteride: Reduced systemic absorption

2. Oral Medications (Prescription Only)

  • • Finasteride/Dutasteride: Blocks DHT (for male pattern hair loss)
  • • Spironolactone: Anti-androgen for female pattern hair loss
  • • Nutraceuticals: Prescription-strength supplements

3. Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) Therapy

One of our most effective hair treatments in Ajmer:

  • • How it works: Your blood is processed to concentrate platelets, then injected into scalp
  • • Growth factors: Stimulate dormant follicles, promote new growth
  • • Procedure: Monthly sessions for 3-4 months, then maintenance
  • • Results: Visible improvement in 3-6 months

4. Laser Hair Therapy

  • • Low-Level Laser Therapy (LLLT): Stimulates cellular activity
  • • In-clinic sessions: Professional laser caps/helmets
  • • Home devices: FDA-cleared laser combs/caps

5. Mesotherapy for Hair

  • • Micro-injections: Nutrients, vitamins, medications directly into scalp
  • • Customized cocktails: Based on your specific deficiency
  • • Improves: Blood circulation, follicle nutrition

6. Hair Transplant (For Advanced Cases)

For permanent hair restoration when other treatments aren't sufficient:

  • • FUE Technique: Follicular Unit Extraction – minimally invasive
  • • Natural results: Hair grows naturally, permanently
  • • Combination approach: Often combined with PRP for better growth

Home Care & Prevention Tips for Healthy Hair

  1. Balanced Diet: Follow tips from our guide on how diet affects hair health
  2. Gentle Hair Care: Avoid heat styling, tight hairstyles, chemical treatments
  3. Regular Scalp Massage: Improves blood circulation, use nourishing oils
  4. Stress Management: Yoga, meditation, adequate sleep (7-8 hours)
  5. Stay Hydrated: Drink at least 2-3 liters of water daily
  6. Protect from Sun: Wear hat/scarf, UV protection for hair
  7. Avoid Smoking: Smoking accelerates hair loss
  8. Regular Trims: Prevents split ends breaking up the hair shaft

Essential Nutrients for Hair Health

  • • Protein: Eggs, chicken, fish, lentils, nuts
  • • Iron: Spinach, beans, red meat, pomegranate
  • • Omega-3: Walnuts, flaxseeds, fish oil
  • • Biotin: Almonds, sweet potatoes, eggs
  • • Vitamin D: Sun exposure, fortified foods, supplements

When to See a Hair Specialist in Ajmer

You should book a consultation at our dermatology clinic in Ajmer if:

  • • You're losing more than 100-150 hairs daily for over 6 weeks
  • • Noticeable thinning or bald patches developing
  • • Hair loss after childbirth that doesn't stop after 6 months
  • • Sudden hair loss at any age
  • • Scalp itching, burning, or pain with hair loss
  • • Family history of baldness and you want preventive treatment
  • • Over-the-counter products haven't helped after 3 months

My Final Advice as Your Hair Specialist

Hair loss can be distressing, but remember – most cases are treatable when addressed properly. The key is:

  • • Don't panic: Stress worsens hair loss
  • • Seek early treatment: Better results when treated early
  • • Be patient: Hair growth is slow – treatments take 3-6 months to show results
  • • Follow through: Consistency with treatment is crucial
  • • Combination approach: Often multiple treatments work best together

At our Ajmer clinic, we create personalized hair restoration plans combining medical treatments, advanced procedures like PRP and laser therapy, and lifestyle modifications. For some patients, we also recommend complementary skin rejuvenation treatments to enhance overall appearance.

For more information on our comprehensive approach to hair and skin health, explore our blogs collection or learn about our other skin treatments.

To your healthy hair and confidence,

Dr. Anju Mehra, MD
Dermatologist & Hair Specialist, Ajmer

title: What Is Laser Skin Treatment? Benefits and Side Effects - Dermatologist Explains
content:
What Is Laser Skin Treatment? Benefits and Side Effects - Expert Guide by Dr. Anju Mehra

Hello, I am Dr. Anju Mehra, a dermatologist and cosmetic skin specialist practicing in Ajmer. In today's world of advanced aesthetics, laser skin treatments have become one of the most sought-after procedures at my clinic. Patients often come with questions about lasers – are they safe? What do they treat? What results can they expect?

Having performed thousands of laser procedures, I've seen how transformative they can be when used correctly for the right indications. This comprehensive guide will demystify laser skin treatments, explaining how they work, their remarkable benefits, and important considerations for anyone considering this advanced skincare solution at our skin and hair clinic in Ajmer.

What's in this article?

  • What Are Laser Skin Treatments?
  • How Do Lasers Work on Skin?
  • Common Types of Skin Lasers
  • What Skin Conditions Can Lasers Treat?
  • Key Benefits of Laser Treatments
  • Popular Laser Procedures
  • Potential Side Effects & Risks
  • Pre-Treatment & Post-Treatment Care
  • Who Is an Ideal Candidate?
  • What to Expect During Consultation

What Are Laser Skin Treatments?

Laser skin treatments use focused beams of light to target specific skin concerns with precision. The term "LASER" stands for Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. Unlike topical creams that work on the surface, lasers penetrate the skin to stimulate healing and regeneration at a cellular level.

  • • Non-invasive to minimally invasive: Most laser treatments don't require cutting or stitches
  • • Targeted approach: Different wavelengths target specific skin components
  • • Medical-grade technology: Should only be performed by trained dermatologists
  • • Customizable: Settings adjusted for your skin type and concern

How Do Lasers Work on Skin? The Science Simplified

Lasers work on the principle of selective photothermolysis. This means they deliver specific light wavelengths that are absorbed by particular targets in your skin (like pigment, water, or hemoglobin), creating controlled damage that triggers your body's natural healing response.

  • • Chromophore targeting: Different colors absorb different light wavelengths
  • • Controlled damage: Microscopic injuries stimulate collagen production
  • • Precision: Surrounding tissues remain largely unaffected
  • • Healing response: Body repairs treated areas with new, healthier skin

Common Types of Skin Lasers

1. Ablative Lasers

Remove thin layers of skin by vaporizing tissue. More aggressive with longer downtime but dramatic results.

  • • CO2 Lasers: Deep resurfacing for scars, wrinkles, growths
  • • Erbium YAG Lasers: Less aggressive than CO2, good for fine lines

2. Non-Ablative Lasers

Heat underlying tissue without damaging surface skin. Minimal downtime, require multiple sessions.

  • • Nd:YAG Lasers: Deep penetration for pigmentation, vascular lesions
  • • Pulsed Dye Lasers: Target blood vessels (rosacea, port-wine stains)
  • • Fractional Lasers: Treat fraction of skin, leaving bridges for faster healing

3. IPL (Intense Pulsed Light)

Not technically a laser (broad spectrum light) but often grouped with laser treatments.

  • • Multiple wavelengths: Treats various concerns simultaneously
  • • Versatile: Good for sun damage, pigmentation, redness

What Skin Conditions Can Lasers Treat?

At our laser treatment center in Ajmer, we use lasers for numerous concerns:

  • • Acne Scars & Active Acne: Lasers can complement traditional acne treatments
  • • Hyperpigmentation: Sun spots, melasma, post-inflammatory marks
  • • Fine Lines & Wrinkles: Stimulate collagen for smoother skin
  • • Uneven Skin Texture: Enlarged pores, rough skin
  • • Vascular Lesions: Spider veins, rosacea, broken capillaries
  • • Hair Removal: Permanent reduction of unwanted hair
  • • Tattoo Removal: Break down ink particles
  • • Skin Tightening: Mild to moderate laxity
  • • Warts & Skin Growths: Benign lesions removal

Key Benefits of Laser Skin Treatments

1. Precision and Targeted Treatment

Lasers can target specific concerns without affecting surrounding skin, unlike chemical peels or topical treatments that affect the entire applied area.

2. Stimulates Natural Collagen Production

The controlled injury triggers your body's healing response, producing new collagen – the protein that keeps skin firm and youthful. This makes lasers superior to temporary fillers for long-term rejuvenation.

3. Minimal Downtime with Advanced Lasers

Modern fractional lasers offer significant results with only 2-3 days of redness, unlike older lasers that required weeks of recovery.

4. Versatility

One laser device can often treat multiple concerns, making it a cost-effective solution for comprehensive skin rejuvenation.

5. Long-Lasting Results

While maintenance is needed, results from laser treatments typically last longer than injectables or topical products, especially for concerns like hair removal or tattoo removal which can be permanent.

6. Complements Other Treatments

Lasers work well with PRP therapy, microneedling, and other procedures for enhanced results.

Popular Laser Procedures at Our Ajmer Clinic

1. Fractional CO2 Laser Resurfacing

  • • Best for: Deep acne scars, severe sun damage, wrinkles
  • • Sessions: 1-2 treatments usually sufficient
  • • Downtime: 5-7 days for complete healing
  • • Results: Dramatic improvement in texture and tone

2. Q-Switched Nd:YAG Laser

  • • Best for: Pigmentation, melasma, tattoo removal
  • • Sessions: 4-6 for pigmentation, 6-10 for tattoos
  • • Downtime: Minimal, slight darkening then flaking

3. Laser Hair Removal

  • • Technology: Diode or Alexandrite lasers
  • • Sessions: 6-8 spaced 4-6 weeks apart
  • • Results: 80-90% permanent reduction
  • • Best for: Dark hair, light skin (though newer lasers work on darker skin)

4. Non-Ablative Fractional Laser

  • • Best for: Maintenance, mild scars, early aging signs
  • • Sessions: 3-5 monthly sessions
  • • Downtime: 24-48 hours of redness
  • • Nickname: "Lunchtime procedure" for minimal downtime

Potential Side Effects & Risks

While generally safe when performed by experts like our team at the best dermatology clinic in Ajmer, lasers do have potential side effects:

Common Temporary Side Effects

  • • Redness & Swelling: Lasts few hours to several days depending on laser
  • • Mild Discomfort: During and after treatment (like sunburn)
  • • Flaking/Peeling: As treated skin sheds (3-7 days)
  • • Temporary Darkening/Lightening: Especially with pigmentation treatments

Less Common Risks (Minimized with Proper Technique)

  • • Infection: Rare with proper aftercare
  • • Scarring: Very rare with modern lasers and experienced operators
  • • Hyperpigmentation/Hypopigmentation: More risk in darker skin types
  • • Herpes Reactivation: Around mouth if prone to cold sores
  • • Eye Injury: Prevented with proper protective eyewear

Pre-Treatment & Post-Treatment Care

Before Your Laser Session

  • • Avoid Sun Exposure: 4 weeks before treatment (use SPF 50+)
  • • Stop Certain Products: Retinoids, glycolic acid 1 week prior
  • • No Tanning: Self-tanners or sunbeds
  • • Shave Area: For hair removal treatments (not wax)
  • • Inform Doctor: Of medications, especially blood thinners

After Your Laser Session

  • • Cool Compresses: Reduce redness and swelling
  • • Gentle Cleansing: Mild cleanser, pat dry (don't rub)
  • • Moisturize Frequently: Healing ointments as recommended
  • • Strict Sun Protection: SPF 50+, hats, avoid sun for 2-4 weeks
  • • Don't Pick or Scratch: Let skin shed naturally
  • • Avoid Heat: Hot showers, saunas, intense exercise for 48 hours

Who Is an Ideal Candidate for Laser Treatment?

Good candidates for laser skin treatments generally:

  • • Have realistic expectations about results
  • • Are in good general health without active infections
  • • Don't have a tan (natural or artificial)
  • • Aren't pregnant or breastfeeding (for most lasers)
  • • Don't have certain medical conditions (like keloid scarring tendency)
  • • Can commit to sun protection before and after
  • • Understand and accept the required downtime

What to Expect During Your Laser Consultation in Ajmer

At my dermatology clinic, every laser journey begins with a thorough consultation:

  1. Skin Analysis: Assessment of your skin type, concerns, and goals
  2. Medical History Review: Medications, allergies, previous treatments
  3. Patch Test: For certain lasers to check skin reaction
  4. Technology Explanation: Which laser is best for your concern
  5. Realistic Expectations: Discussion of probable outcomes, sessions needed
  6. Cost Estimate: Transparent pricing for complete treatment plan
  7. Pre-care Instructions: How to prepare for optimal results
  8. Q&A Session: All your questions answered thoroughly

My Final Thoughts as Your Dermatologist

Laser skin treatments represent a powerful tool in modern dermatology when used appropriately. The key to successful laser treatment is:

  • • Proper diagnosis: Not every skin concern needs laser
  • • Right technology: Matching laser to your specific concern and skin type
  • • Experienced operator: Settings make all the difference
  • • Realistic expectations: Lasers improve but don't perfect
  • • Proper aftercare: Crucial for optimal results and safety

Sometimes, combining laser with other skin treatments like chemical peels or microneedling yields the best outcome. Each patient receives a customized plan at our clinic.

Remember, while lasers are medical devices, they're not magic wands. They require expertise to use safely and effectively. I've seen patients get life-changing results from laser treatments, but I've also seen complications from improperly performed procedures.

For more information on maintaining healthy skin, explore our guide on how diet affects skin health or browse our complete blogs collection.

To your radiant, healthy skin,

Dr. Anju Mehra, MD
Dermatologist & Cosmetic Skin Specialist, Ajmer

title: Hair Fall Reasons in Men & Women and Medical Solutions - Dermatologist Explains
content:
Hair Fall Reasons in Men & Women and Medical Solutions - Expert Guide by Dr. Anju Mehra

Hello, I am Dr. Anju Mehra, a dermatologist and hair restoration specialist practicing in Ajmer. In my daily practice at the clinic, hair fall remains one of the most common concerns I encounter among both men and women. The distress it causes goes beyond physical appearance, affecting confidence and emotional well-being.

Having treated thousands of hair loss patients, I've witnessed how understanding the root cause is the first step toward effective treatment. This comprehensive guide will explain the different reasons behind hair fall in men and women, and the advanced medical solutions available at our hair treatment center in Ajmer.

What's in this article?

  • Normal vs Excessive Hair Fall
  • Hair Fall Reasons in Men
  • Hair Fall Reasons in Women
  • Common Causes in Both Genders
  • How We Diagnose Hair Fall
  • Medical Solutions & Treatments
  • PRP for Hair Regrowth
  • Hair Transplant Explained
  • Lifestyle & Prevention
  • Common Myths Debunked

Normal Hair Fall vs Excessive Hair Fall: Understanding the Difference

Before panicking, it's important to understand what's normal:

  • • Normal Shedding: 50-100 hairs per day is normal hair cycle
  • • Excessive Hair Fall: Clumps of hair, thinning patches, widening part
  • • Duration Matters: More than 3 months of increased shedding needs evaluation
  • • Scalp Visibility: Visible scalp through hair is a red flag

Hair Fall Reasons in Men: More Than Just Genetics

1. Male Pattern Baldness (Androgenetic Alopecia)

Affects 50% of men by age 50, following predictable patterns (receding hairline, crown thinning).

  • • Causes: Genetic sensitivity to DHT (dihydrotestosterone)
  • • Pattern: Hamilton-Norwood scale (stages I-VII)
  • • Onset: Can begin as early as late teens

2. Alopecia Areata

Autoimmune condition causing patchy hair loss.

  • • Appearance: Round, smooth bald patches
  • • Associated: Often with stress or other autoimmune conditions

3. Traction Alopecia

Common in men who wear tight hairstyles or turbans.

4. Medications & Treatments

Certain medications for blood pressure, cholesterol, depression, and cancer treatments.

Hair Fall Reasons in Women: The Multifactorial Challenge

1. Female Pattern Hair Loss (FPHL)

Affects 40% of women by age 50, different pattern than men.

  • • Pattern: Ludwig scale - diffuse thinning, widening part
  • • Hairline: Usually preserved unlike in men
  • • Hormonal: Androgen sensitivity plus other factors

2. Telogen Effluvium

Temporary but dramatic hair shedding 2-3 months after triggering event.

  • • Triggers: Childbirth (postpartum), surgery, severe illness, crash diets
  • • Recovery: Usually resolves in 6-9 months if trigger is removed

3. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)

Hormonal imbalance causing thinning hair, often with acne and irregular periods.

4. Postmenopausal Hair Loss

Estrogen decline and relative androgen increase leads to thinning.

5. Traction & Styling Damage

From tight ponytails, braids, heat styling, and chemical treatments.

Common Causes in Both Men & Women

1. Nutritional Deficiencies

  • • Iron: Most common deficiency causing hair loss
  • • Vitamin D: Essential for hair follicle cycling
  • • Vitamin B12: Especially in vegetarians
  • • Protein: Hair is 90% protein (keratin)
  • • Zinc: Deficiency causes telogen effluvium

2. Thyroid Disorders

Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism can cause diffuse hair loss.

3. Chronic Stress

Physical or emotional stress pushes hair into resting phase.

4. Scalp Conditions

  • • Seborrheic Dermatitis: Inflammation and scaling
  • • Psoriasis: Thick plaques that affect hair growth
  • • Fungal Infections: Like tinea capitis

5. Autoimmune Diseases

Lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, etc., can cause hair loss.

6. Medications

Blood thinners, retinoids, antidepressants, gout medications.

How We Diagnose Hair Fall at Our Ajmer Clinic

Accurate diagnosis is crucial for effective treatment. At our dermatology clinic in Ajmer, we use:

  1. Detailed History: Onset, pattern, family history, medications, diet, stress
  2. Hair Pull Test: Gently pulling hair to assess shedding
  3. Trichoscopy: Magnified scalp examination (digital microscope)
  4. Blood Tests: Thyroid, iron studies, vitamin D, hormones, CBC
  5. Scalp Biopsy: Rarely needed for difficult diagnoses
  6. Hair Wash Test: Counting shed hairs during washing

Medical Solutions & Treatments for Hair Fall

1. FDA-Approved Medications

Minoxidil (Topical)
  • • For: Men and women
  • • Strength: 5% for men, 2-5% for women
  • • Mechanism: Vasodilator, prolongs growth phase
  • • Note: Initial shedding common, lifelong use needed
Finasteride (Oral)
  • • For: Men only (contraindicated in women of childbearing age)
  • • Mechanism: Blocks DHT conversion
  • • Effectiveness: 80-90% stop further loss, 65% regrow hair
  • • Prescription: Requires dermatologist prescription
Dutasteride

More potent DHT blocker, used off-label for resistant cases.

Spironolactone (For Women)

Anti-androgen for female pattern hair loss, especially with PCOS.

2. Low Level Laser Therapy (LLLT)

  • • Technology: Laser caps, helmets, in-clinic devices
  • • Mechanism: Stimulates cellular activity in follicles
  • • FDA Cleared: For both men and women
  • • Results: Visible in 3-6 months

3. Topical Formulations

Custom compounded solutions combining minoxidil with other agents like finasteride, dutasteride, or growth factors.

PRP (Platelet-Rich Plasma) Therapy for Hair Regrowth

One of the most effective treatments we offer at our PRP therapy center in Ajmer:

  • • Process: Patient's blood spun to concentrate platelets, injected into scalp
  • • Growth Factors: Stimulate dormant hair follicles
  • • Sessions: 3-6 initial sessions monthly, then maintenance
  • • Best For: Early to moderate hair loss, thinning areas
  • • Combination: Works well with minoxidil and other treatments

Hair Transplant: Permanent Solution for Baldness

For advanced hair loss, we perform hair transplant in Ajmer using modern techniques:

FUE (Follicular Unit Extraction)

  • • Technique: Individual follicles extracted from donor area
  • • Scarring: Tiny dot scars (not visible with short hair)
  • • Recovery: 5-7 days, faster than FUT

FUT (Follicular Unit Transplantation)

  • • Technique: Strip of scalp removed, dissected into grafts
  • • Graft Count: Higher number in one session
  • • Linear Scar: Can be hidden with hair

Post-Transplant Care

  • • Medications: Continue finasteride/minoxidil to protect native hair
  • • Growth Timeline: Shedding at 2-4 weeks, growth from 3-4 months
  • • Final Results: 12-18 months

Lifestyle & Preventive Measures

Dietary Recommendations

  • • Protein: Eggs, fish, chicken, lentils, dairy
  • • Iron: Spinach, red meat, pomegranate, dates
  • • Biotin: Nuts, whole grains, bananas
  • • Zinc: Pumpkin seeds, chickpeas, mushrooms
  • • Omega-3: Flaxseeds, walnuts, fish

Hair Care Practices

  • • Gentle Handling: Avoid tight hairstyles, rough towel drying
  • • Heat Protection: Limit blow drying, flat iron use
  • • Chemical Treatments: Space out coloring/straightening
  • • Scalp Massage: Improves blood circulation

Stress Management

  • • Exercise: Regular physical activity reduces stress hormones
  • • Sleep: 7-8 hours quality sleep for hair repair
  • • Meditation/Yoga: Reduces cortisol levels

Common Hair Fall Myths Debunked

  • • Myth: Washing hair daily causes hair fall
  • • Truth: You lose the hair that was already destined to fall
  • • Myth: Only men get hereditary baldness
  • • Truth: Women get it too, but pattern is different
  • • Myth: Hair oils can regrow hair
  • • Truth: Oils improve scalp health but don't reverse genetic hair loss
  • • Myth: Cutting hair makes it grow thicker
  • • Truth: Hair thickness is determined at follicle level
  • • Myth: Stress turns hair grey but doesn't cause hair fall
  • • Truth: Stress is a major cause of telogen effluvium

When to See a Dermatologist for Hair Fall

  • • Sudden or patchy hair loss
  • • Hair loss for more than 3 months
  • • Visible scalp or widening part
  • • Hair loss with itching, pain, or scaling
  • • Family history of baldness (early intervention is key)
  • • Hair loss after starting new medication

My Final Thoughts as Your Hair Specialist

Hair fall treatment has evolved dramatically in recent years. The most important principles I emphasize to my patients at our skin and hair clinic in Ajmer are:

  • • Early Intervention: Better to treat when 30% lost than 70%
  • • Correct Diagnosis: Treatment differs vastly by cause
  • • Combination Therapy: Often 2-3 treatments work better than one
  • • Realistic Expectations: Regrowth takes 3-6 months, maintenance is lifelong
  • • Patience & Consistency: Hair treatments require commitment

Remember, hair loss is a medical condition, not just a cosmetic concern. With proper diagnosis and treatment, most hair loss can be successfully managed. I've helped countless patients regain not just their hair but their confidence.

For more information on related treatments, you might find our guide on laser skin treatments helpful, or browse our complete educational blogs.

To your healthy hair journey,

Dr. Anju Mehra, MD
Dermatologist & Hair Restoration Specialist, Ajmer

title: Best Acne Treatment Options Recommended by Dermatologists
content:
Best Acne Treatment Options Recommended by Dermatologists

Acne is one of the most common skin concerns affecting teenagers and adults alike. From painful pimples to stubborn acne scars, it can impact not only your skin but also your confidence. The good news? Dermatologists today offer advanced, safe, and highly effective acne treatments tailored to your skin type and severity.

If you’re struggling with persistent breakouts in Ajmer, consulting an experienced dermatologist like Dr. Anju Mehra can help you get the right diagnosis and personalized treatment plan at our advanced skin treatment clinic in Ajmer.

What Causes Acne?

Acne develops when hair follicles become clogged with oil (sebum), dead skin cells, and bacteria. Several factors contribute to this condition:

  • Excess oil production
  • Hormonal imbalance
  • Stress
  • Poor skincare routine
  • Genetic factors
  • Use of comedogenic cosmetics
  • Unhealthy diet

Understanding the root cause is essential because acne treatment for hormonal imbalance is not "one-size-fits-all."

Types of Acne

Before starting treatment, dermatologists classify acne into different types:

  • Whiteheads
  • Blackheads
  • Papules
  • Pustules
  • Nodules
  • Cystic acne

Mild acne may respond to topical creams, while moderate to severe acne may require oral medications or advanced laser skin treatments.

Best Acne Treatment Options Recommended by Dermatologists

1. Topical Medications (First-Line Treatment)

Topical treatments are usually the first step in managing mild to moderate acne.

  • Retinoids (Tretinoin, Adapalene) – Unclog pores and prevent new acne.
  • Benzoyl Peroxide – Kills acne-causing bacteria.
  • Salicylic Acid – Reduces inflammation and clears pores.
  • Topical Antibiotics – Control bacterial growth.

If you are unsure which cream suits your skin type, visit our dermatology consultation page for expert guidance.

2. Oral Medications for Moderate to Severe Acne

When topical treatments aren’t enough, dermatologists may prescribe oral medications.

  • Oral Antibiotics – Reduce bacteria and inflammation.
  • Isotretinoin treatment – Highly effective for severe, cystic acne.
  • Hormonal Therapy (for women) – Birth control pills or anti-androgen medications.

3. Chemical Peels for Acne & Pigmentation

Chemical peel treatment uses safe acids like glycolic acid or salicylic acid to:

  • Remove dead skin cells
  • Reduce acne marks
  • Improve skin texture
  • Control oil production

4. Laser & Light-Based Acne Treatments

Advanced dermatology clinics now offer laser treatments for acne to treat both active acne and acne scars.

  • Reduces bacteria
  • Controls oil glands
  • Improves acne scars
  • Quick recovery time

5. Acne Scar Treatments

Once acne heals, scars can remain. Dermatologists recommend:

  • Microneedling treatment
  • PRP (Platelet-Rich Plasma)
  • Laser resurfacing
  • Dermal fillers for deep scars

6. Lifestyle & Skincare Modifications

  • Use non-comedogenic products
  • Avoid over-washing the face
  • Reduce sugary and oily foods
  • Manage stress levels
  • Avoid touching or popping pimples

If you experience seasonal skin issues, read our guide on skin allergies during weather change.

Why You Should Avoid Self-Medication

Many people try random creams suggested by friends or social media influencers. This can worsen acne, cause irritation, and increase pigmentation. Proper evaluation by a dermatologist ensures safe and effective treatment. Learn more about our clinical skin treatments in Ajmer.

How Long Does Acne Treatment Take?

  • 4–6 weeks for visible improvement
  • 3–6 months for significant control
  • Longer duration for severe acne

When Should You See a Dermatologist?

  • Acne is painful or cystic
  • Over-the-counter products are not working
  • Acne is leaving scars
  • Sudden adult-onset acne
  • Acne affects your confidence

Acne Treatment in Ajmer – Expert Care You Can Trust

If you're looking for advanced and personalized acne treatment in Ajmer, book an appointment with Dr. Anju Mehra today. Explore our full range of dermatology services in Ajmer.

Final Thoughts

Acne is treatable — and you don’t have to live with it. With modern dermatology advancements, clearer and healthier skin is achievable at any age. Early diagnosis, proper treatment, and consistent follow-ups are the key to long-term results.

title: Skin Allergies During Weather Change: Symptoms & Treatment
content:
Skin Allergies During Weather Change: Causes, Symptoms & Treatment Guide

Weather change is one of the most common triggers for skin allergies. As seasons shift—from summer to monsoon or winter to spring—our skin struggles to adapt to sudden changes in temperature, humidity, dust levels, and pollen exposure.

Many people in Ajmer and nearby areas experience itching, rashes, dryness, redness, or fungal infections during seasonal transitions. If your skin reacts every time the weather changes, this detailed guide will help you understand the causes, symptoms, and best treatment options available.

Why Do Skin Allergies Increase During Weather Change?

Seasonal changes directly affect your skin’s protective barrier. When this barrier weakens, allergens and irritants can penetrate more easily.

1. Sudden Temperature Fluctuations

Rapid shifts from hot to cold or humid to dry air disturb the skin’s natural oil balance, leading to irritation and redness.

2. Low Humidity in Winter

Cold air strips moisture from your skin, causing dryness, flaking, and itching.

3. Increased Pollen in Spring

Pollens released by plants and trees can trigger allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.

4. Dust & Pollution

Strong winds increase dust exposure, irritating exposed skin and triggering inflammation.

5. Fungal Growth in Humid Weather

During monsoon, high moisture levels create a favorable environment for fungal infections.

Common Symptoms of Seasonal Skin Allergies

  • Persistent itching
  • Red rashes or raised bumps
  • Dry, flaky skin
  • Swelling in affected areas
  • Burning sensation
  • Small blisters
  • Hives (urticaria)
  • Patchy skin discoloration

If symptoms last more than a few days or worsen, medical consultation is essential.

Types of Skin Allergies During Weather Change

1. Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)

Often worsens in winter due to dryness. Skin becomes inflamed, itchy, and cracked.

2. Contact Dermatitis

Triggered by allergens like dust, pollen, or certain fabrics.

3. Urticaria (Hives)

Sudden red, raised itchy patches caused by allergic reactions.

4. Fungal Infections

Common in humid weather, especially in skin folds.

Who Is More at Risk?

  • People with sensitive skin
  • Individuals with asthma or allergic rhinitis
  • Children and elderly
  • Those with a history of eczema
  • People exposed to dust and pollution regularly

Effective Treatment Options

1. Moisturizers

Use fragrance-free moisturizers regularly to maintain hydration.

2. Antihistamines

Help reduce itching and allergic reactions.

3. Topical Steroid Creams

Prescribed for moderate to severe inflammation under medical supervision.

4. Antifungal Creams

Effective during monsoon-related fungal infections.

5. Gentle Skincare Routine

Use mild cleansers and lukewarm water for bathing.

Home Remedies for Mild Cases

  • Applying aloe vera gel
  • Using cold compress for itching relief
  • Taking oatmeal baths
  • Staying hydrated
  • Wearing breathable cotton clothes

Preventive Tips During Seasonal Change

  • Apply sunscreen even in winter
  • Use a humidifier during dry seasons
  • Avoid harsh chemical products
  • Keep skin moisturized twice daily
  • Maintain personal hygiene
  • Change sweaty clothes immediately
  • Keep nails trimmed

When Should You See a Doctor?

  • Rash spreads rapidly
  • Severe swelling occurs
  • Skin becomes infected (pus, fever)
  • Symptoms do not improve within 3–5 days
  • Frequent recurrence during every season change

Expert Skin Care in Ajmer

If you are struggling with seasonal skin allergies, consult an experienced dermatologist for accurate diagnosis and personalized treatment.

Dr. Anju Mehra provides comprehensive evaluation and advanced treatment for eczema, fungal infections, urticaria, and chronic skin allergies.

Skin allergies during weather change are common but manageable. Early recognition, proper skincare, and timely medical consultation can significantly reduce flare-ups and prevent complications.

title: Seasonal Skincare Tips to Prevent Rashes and Itching
content:
Seasonal Skincare Tips to Prevent Rashes and Itching – Expert Guide by Dr. Anju Mehra

Skin problems like rashes, redness, dryness, and itching are very common during seasonal changes. Whether it’s the harsh summer heat, monsoon humidity, or dry winter air, each season affects your skin differently. Many people in Ajmer experience frequent itching and irritation simply because they do not adjust their skincare routine according to the weather.

In this detailed guide, Dr. Anju Mehra, Dermatologist in Ajmer, shares practical and effective seasonal skincare tips to help you prevent rashes and itching throughout the year.

Why Do Seasonal Changes Cause Rashes and Itching?

Our skin acts as a protective barrier. When the weather changes, the moisture level, temperature, and humidity in the air also change. This affects the skin barrier and can lead to:

  • Dryness and flaking
  • Fungal infections
  • Allergic reactions
  • Heat rashes
  • Increased sensitivity
  • Excess sweating and clogged pores

If not managed properly, minor irritation can turn into persistent skin conditions.

🌞 Summer Skincare Tips to Prevent Rashes & Itching

Ajmer summers are extremely hot and dry. Excess sweating combined with dust can cause itching and red rashes.

1. Keep Your Skin Cool and Dry

  • ✔ Take 2 baths daily if needed
  • ✔ Use mild, fragrance-free soap
  • ✔ Pat dry instead of rubbing the skin

2. Wear Breathable Clothing

  • ✔ Choose loose cotton clothes
  • ✔ Avoid tight innerwear

3. Stay Hydrated

  • ✔ Drink 8–10 glasses of water daily
  • ✔ Include coconut water and seasonal fruits

4. Use Sunscreen Daily

  • ✔ Use SPF 30+ sunscreen
  • ✔ Reapply every 3–4 hours

5. Avoid Heavy Creams

Use light, gel-based moisturizers instead of thick creams during summer.

🌧 Monsoon Skincare Tips to Prevent Fungal Infections

Humidity during monsoon creates a perfect environment for fungal infections and itching.

1. Keep Skin Dry

  • ✔ Dry feet and body folds properly
  • ✔ Change wet clothes immediately

2. Use Antifungal Powder (If Needed)

Especially if you sweat excessively.

3. Avoid Sharing Towels & Clothes

Fungal infections spread easily.

4. Cleanse Gently

Avoid harsh scrubbing as it damages the skin barrier.

5. Maintain Foot Hygiene

Wash and dry feet thoroughly, especially between toes.

❄ Winter Skincare Tips to Prevent Dryness & Itching

Winter dryness is one of the biggest causes of itching and eczema flare-ups.

1. Moisturize Immediately After Bath

Apply a thick moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing to lock in moisture.

2. Avoid Hot Showers

Very hot water removes natural oils and increases dryness.

3. Use a Humidifier

Indoor heating makes air dry. A humidifier helps maintain skin moisture.

4. Choose Gentle Cleansers

Avoid soaps that create excessive foam.

5. Apply Lip Balm & Hand Cream

Hands and lips are most affected during winter.

Common Skin Conditions During Seasonal Changes

1. Heat Rash (Prickly Heat)

Common in summer due to blocked sweat glands.

2. Fungal Infection

Common in monsoon due to humidity.

3. Eczema

Flare-ups are common in winter due to dryness.

4. Allergic Dermatitis

Can occur in any season due to allergens.

If itching lasts more than 5–7 days or spreads rapidly, consult a skin specialist immediately.

General Year-Round Skincare Tips

  • ✔ Avoid scratching itchy areas
  • ✔ Keep nails short
  • ✔ Use hypoallergenic products
  • ✔ Wash bedsheets weekly
  • ✔ Eat a balanced diet rich in vitamins
  • ✔ Avoid self-medication with steroid creams

When Should You See a Doctor?

  • Severe itching disturbing sleep
  • Redness with swelling
  • Pus or fluid discharge
  • Recurring rashes
  • Dark patches after itching

Early diagnosis prevents complications and long-term skin damage.

Healthy Skin Starts with Seasonal Awareness

Your skin’s needs change with the weather. Using the same products throughout the year may not work. A simple adjustment in your skincare routine according to season can prevent most rashes and itching problems.

Taking preventive steps is always better than treating infections later.

If you are experiencing persistent rashes, itching, fungal infections, or seasonal allergies, consult an experienced dermatologist for proper evaluation and treatment.

title: Why Do Pimples Keep Coming Back? Dermatologist Explains Causes & Treatment
content:
Why Do Pimples Keep Coming Back? Dermatologist Explains Causes & Treatment

Pimples are one of the most common skin concerns faced by teenagers and adults alike. Many people treat acne with creams or home remedies, and the pimples may disappear for some time. However, a very common question patients ask is: Why do pimples keep coming back?

If you are also struggling with recurring acne, you are not alone. According to dermatology experts, pimples can come back due to multiple internal and external factors. Understanding the real causes can help you choose the right acne treatment in Ajmer and prevent future breakouts.

In this article, we will discuss why pimples return frequently, the common mistakes people make, and effective dermatologist-recommended treatments.

What Are Pimples?

Pimples, also known as acne, occur when hair follicles on the skin become clogged with oil, dead skin cells, and bacteria. This blockage leads to inflammation and the formation of whiteheads, blackheads, papules, pustules, or cystic acne.

Acne most commonly appears on the:

  • Face
  • Forehead
  • Nose
  • Chin
  • Chest
  • Back

While acne is very common during teenage years, many adults continue to experience breakouts due to lifestyle, hormonal changes, and skin care habits.

Why Do Pimples Keep Coming Back?

There are several reasons why pimples keep returning even after treatment.

1. Excess Oil Production

One of the biggest causes of recurring pimples is overactive sebaceous glands that produce too much oil (sebum). Excess oil mixes with dead skin cells and blocks pores, creating the perfect environment for acne. People with oily skin are more likely to experience repeated breakouts.

2. Hormonal Imbalance

Hormones play a major role in acne development. Hormonal changes increase oil production, which leads to clogged pores. Common situations where hormones trigger pimples include:

  • Teenage puberty
  • Menstrual cycle
  • PCOS (Polycystic Ovary Syndrome)
  • Stress-related hormonal changes

This is why many women experience pimples around the chin and jawline during periods.

3. Using the Wrong Skincare Products

Using products that are too oily or comedogenic (pore-clogging) can worsen acne. Many people unknowingly use:

  • Heavy moisturizers
  • Oil-based makeup
  • Harsh face washes

These products can irritate the skin and cause repeated pimples. Dermatologists recommend non-comedogenic and oil-free skincare products for acne-prone skin.

4. Poor Skin Cleansing Routine

Not cleaning your skin properly allows dirt, sweat, oil, and pollution to accumulate on the skin surface. Common mistakes include:

  • Not washing the face after sweating
  • Sleeping with makeup on
  • Overwashing the face (which damages the skin barrier)

A simple twice-daily cleansing routine is essential to prevent breakouts.

5. Stress and Lifestyle Factors

Stress increases the production of cortisol, a hormone that can stimulate oil glands and worsen acne. Lifestyle habits that can contribute to pimples include:

  • Lack of sleep
  • Unhealthy diet
  • Excess sugar consumption
  • Smoking

Maintaining a balanced lifestyle can significantly improve skin health.

6. Touching or Picking Pimples

Many people develop a habit of touching or popping pimples. This spreads bacteria and causes more inflammation. Picking pimples can also lead to:

  • Acne scars
  • Skin infections
  • Dark spots

Dermatologists strongly advise never squeezing pimples. If you have scars, you may need laser treatment for acne scars.

7. Bacterial Growth

The bacteria Cutibacterium acnes plays an important role in acne formation. When pores are clogged, this bacteria multiplies rapidly, causing redness and swelling. Without proper treatment, the infection can return again and again.

Common Mistakes That Make Pimples Worse

According to dermatology specialists, many patients unknowingly make mistakes that trigger recurring acne. Some of these include:

  • Trying too many skincare products at once
  • Using harsh scrubs on inflamed skin
  • Following random home remedies from the internet
  • Stopping treatment too early
  • Ignoring professional medical advice

Acne treatment requires consistency and patience to see long-term results.

Effective Dermatologist-Recommended Treatments

The right treatment depends on the type and severity of acne. A dermatologist may recommend the following options.

1. Topical Medications

These are creams or gels applied directly to the skin. Common ingredients include:

  • Retinoids
  • Benzoyl peroxide
  • Salicylic acid
  • Antibiotic creams

These medications help unclog pores, reduce bacteria, and control oil production.

2. Oral Medications

For moderate to severe acne, doctors may prescribe oral medicines such as:

  • Antibiotics
  • Hormonal therapy (for women)
  • Isotretinoin (in severe cases)

These medications treat acne from the inside and reduce recurring breakouts.

3. Chemical Peels

Chemical peels help remove dead skin cells and improve skin texture. They also reduce acne marks and prevent clogged pores. This treatment is commonly used in dermatology clinics.

4. Laser and Light Therapy

Advanced treatments like laser therapy can reduce acne-causing bacteria and inflammation. These procedures are especially helpful for persistent or treatment-resistant acne. You can read more about the best acne treatment options recommended by dermatologists.

5. Proper Skincare Routine

A dermatologist-approved skincare routine usually includes:

  • Gentle cleanser (twice daily)
  • Oil-free moisturizer
  • Sunscreen (SPF 30 or higher)
  • Acne treatment products

Consistency with skincare is essential for long-term results.

Tips to Prevent Pimples from Coming Back

Here are some simple tips recommended by Dr. Anju Mehra to prevent recurring acne:

  • ✔ Wash your face twice daily with a gentle cleanser
  • ✔ Use non-comedogenic skincare products
  • ✔ Avoid touching or popping pimples
  • ✔ Maintain a healthy diet and drink enough water
  • ✔ Manage stress through exercise or meditation
  • ✔ Remove makeup before sleeping
  • ✔ Consult a dermatologist if acne persists

These small habits can make a big difference in maintaining clear skin.

When Should You See a Dermatologist?

If your pimples:

  • Keep coming back frequently
  • Leave scars or dark spots
  • Do not improve with over-the-counter treatments
  • Become painful or cystic

then it is important to seek professional help. A qualified dermatologist can diagnose the underlying cause and create a personalized treatment plan for your skin.

Final Thoughts

Recurring pimples can be frustrating and affect self-confidence. However, understanding the root causes and following proper treatment can significantly reduce breakouts. Instead of experimenting with random products, it is always better to consult a skin specialist. With the right guidance and skincare routine, achieving healthier and clearer skin is absolutely possible.

title: Best Treatments for Pigmentation and Dark Spots
content:
Best Treatments for Pigmentation and Dark Spots

Skin pigmentation and dark spots are common skin concerns that affect people of all ages and skin types. These issues can appear as patches of darker skin, freckles, sunspots, or marks left behind by acne. While they are usually harmless, many people seek treatment to achieve a more even and radiant complexion. Understanding the causes of pigmentation and the available treatments can help you choose the best solution for your skin.

What is Skin Pigmentation?

Skin pigmentation refers to the coloring of the skin, which is determined by a pigment called melanin. Melanin is produced by specialized cells known as melanocytes. When the body produces excess melanin in certain areas, it can lead to hyperpigmentation, which appears as dark spots or patches on the skin.

Hyperpigmentation can develop on the face, neck, hands, and other parts of the body that are frequently exposed to the sun. While it is not dangerous, it can affect a person’s confidence and overall skin appearance.

Common Causes of Pigmentation and Dark Spots

Several factors can trigger the development of pigmentation and dark spots. Some of the most common causes include:

  1. Sun Exposure: Prolonged exposure to ultraviolet (UV) rays from the sun stimulates melanin production in the skin. This can result in sunspots or age spots, especially on areas like the face, shoulders, and hands.
  2. Hormonal Changes: Hormonal fluctuations can cause a type of pigmentation called melasma, which often appears as dark patches on the cheeks, forehead, and upper lip. This is commonly seen during pregnancy or while taking hormonal medications.
  3. Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation: Skin injuries such as acne, burns, cuts, or allergic reactions can leave behind dark spots after the skin heals. This condition is known as post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation.
  4. Aging: As the skin ages, it becomes more prone to uneven pigmentation and the appearance of dark spots.
  5. Certain Medications: Some medications can increase skin sensitivity to sunlight, leading to pigmentation.

Understanding the cause of pigmentation is important because it helps dermatologists determine the most effective treatment plan.

Best Treatments for Pigmentation and Dark Spots

There are several effective treatments available to reduce pigmentation and improve skin tone. The best treatment depends on the severity of the pigmentation, skin type, and underlying cause.

1. Topical Treatments

Dermatologists often recommend medicated creams or serums to lighten pigmentation. Some commonly used ingredients include:

  • Vitamin C – Helps brighten the skin and reduce dark spots.
  • Retinoids – Promote skin cell turnover and improve skin texture.
  • Kojic Acid – Reduces melanin production in the skin.
  • Niacinamide – Helps fade pigmentation and improve skin tone.
  • Hydroquinone – A powerful skin-lightening agent used under medical supervision.

Regular use of these topical treatments can gradually reduce the appearance of pigmentation.

2. Chemical Peels

Chemical peels are a popular dermatological treatment used to remove the outer layer of the skin. During this procedure, a chemical solution is applied to the skin to exfoliate damaged skin cells.

Chemical peels help reduce dark spots, improve skin texture, and promote new skin cell growth. Mild to medium chemical peels can significantly improve pigmentation with minimal downtime.

3. Laser Treatment

Laser therapy is one of the most advanced treatments for pigmentation and dark spots. The laser targets the melanin in the skin and breaks it down, allowing the body to gradually remove the pigment.

Benefits of laser treatment include precise targeting of pigmentation, faster results compared to topical treatments, and improved skin tone and clarity. Multiple sessions may be required depending on the severity of pigmentation.

4. Microdermabrasion

Microdermabrasion is a minimally invasive treatment that exfoliates the skin using a specialized device. It removes the outer layer of dead skin cells and stimulates collagen production. This treatment helps reduce mild pigmentation, improve skin brightness, and enhance overall skin texture.

5. Prescription Medications

In some cases, dermatologists may prescribe oral medications or stronger topical formulations to treat stubborn pigmentation. These treatments should always be used under professional supervision to avoid side effects.

Importance of Sunscreen

Regardless of the treatment you choose, sunscreen is essential for preventing further pigmentation. Sun exposure can worsen dark spots and reduce the effectiveness of treatments.

Dermatologists recommend:

  • Using a broad-spectrum sunscreen with SPF 30 or higher
  • Reapplying sunscreen every 2–3 hours when outdoors
  • Wearing protective clothing and hats when exposed to the sun

Consistent sun protection helps maintain treatment results and prevents new pigmentation from forming.

Skincare Tips to Prevent Pigmentation

  • Cleanse your skin gently twice a day.
  • Use products with antioxidants like Vitamin C.
  • Avoid picking or squeezing acne, as it can cause dark marks.
  • Exfoliate regularly to remove dead skin cells.
  • Keep your skin hydrated with a good moisturizer.
  • Protect your skin from excessive sun exposure.

When to See a Dermatologist

If pigmentation or dark spots persist despite using over-the-counter products, it is best to consult a dermatologist. A skin specialist can properly diagnose the condition and recommend the most suitable treatment for your skin type. Professional treatments not only provide faster results but also ensure that your skin remains healthy and safe during the process.

Conclusion

Pigmentation and dark spots are common skin concerns, but they can be effectively treated with the right approach. Treatments such as topical creams, chemical peels, laser therapy, and microdermabrasion can significantly improve skin tone and reduce dark spots. Along with professional treatments, proper skincare and daily sun protection play a crucial role in maintaining healthy and even-toned skin.

title: Acne (Pimples): Complete Guide by an Experienced Dermatologist
content:
Acne (Pimples): Complete Guide by an Experienced Dermatologist

✍️ Introduction

Acne is one of the most common skin conditions affecting both teenagers and adults. In my 15 years of clinical experience, I’ve seen many patients struggle not just with acne, but with misinformation and ineffective treatments that often make things worse.

This guide will help you understand acne properly and choose the right approach for treatment.

🔍 What Causes Acne?

Acne develops due to multiple factors:

  • • Hormonal Changes: Common during puberty, menstruation, pregnancy, or stress
  • • Excess Oil Production (Sebum): Oily skin increases the chances of clogged pores
  • • Bacterial Growth (P. acnes): Leads to inflammation and breakouts
  • • Clogged Pores: Dead skin cells block hair follicles
  • • Lifestyle Factors: Poor diet, stress, and lack of sleep
  • • Wrong Skincare Products: Comedogenic products can worsen acne

❌ Common Myths About Acne

  • • ❌ “Eating chocolate always causes acne”
  • • ❌ “Washing your face frequently will cure acne”
  • • ❌ “Toothpaste can dry out pimples effectively”
  • • ❌ “Sun exposure clears acne permanently”

👉 These are myths. Following them can actually damage your skin.

✅ Most Effective Acne Treatments

1. 🧼 Proper Skincare Routine

  • • Use a gentle cleanser twice daily
  • • Avoid overwashing or harsh scrubs

2. 💊 Medical Treatments

  • • Topical treatments (Retinoids, Benzoyl Peroxide)
  • • Oral medications (Antibiotics, Hormonal therapy) (Always consult a dermatologist before use)

3. 🥗 Diet & Lifestyle

  • • Reduce junk and oily food
  • • Stay hydrated
  • • Maintain proper sleep

4. 🧪 Advanced Dermatological Treatments

  • • Chemical Peels
  • • Laser Therapy
  • • Acne Scar Treatments

⚠️ When Should You See a Dermatologist?

  • • Acne becomes severe or painful
  • • Scarring starts to appear
  • • Over-the-counter treatments don’t work
  • • You have cystic acne

💡 Expert Tips (From 15 Years of Practice)

  • • Always choose products based on your skin type
  • • Avoid blindly following home remedies
  • • Be patient—acne treatment takes time
  • • Regular consultations can prevent long-term damage

🏁 Conclusion

Acne is a manageable and treatable condition, but the key lies in the right knowledge and professional guidance. Early and proper treatment can prevent long-term scars and improve overall skin health.

title: Why Skin Gets Darker in Summer – Dermatologist Guide
heading: Why Skin Gets Darker in Summer – Causes & Prevention Tips
content:
Why Skin Gets Darker in Summer – Dermatologist Guide

Many people notice their skin becoming darker during summer. This is mainly due to increased sun exposure and higher UV radiation levels. While tanning is a natural protective response of the skin, excessive exposure can lead to pigmentation and long-term skin damage.

Why Skin Gets Darker in Summer

During summer, the sun emits stronger ultraviolet (UV) rays. These rays stimulate melanin production in the skin. Melanin is the pigment responsible for skin color. When melanin increases, the skin appears darker.

This process is the body's natural defense mechanism against sun damage.

What is Skin Tanning

Skin tanning occurs when UV rays penetrate the skin and trigger melanocytes to produce more melanin. This extra melanin spreads across the skin, causing darkening.

Although tanning may look temporary, repeated exposure can cause permanent pigmentation.

Common Causes of Summer Darkening

  • Direct sun exposure
  • Not using sunscreen
  • Outdoor activities without protection
  • Heat and dehydration
  • Sweating and clogged pores
  • Using harsh skincare products

Who is at Higher Risk

  • People working outdoors
  • Students and travelers
  • Athletes
  • People with sensitive skin
  • Individuals not using sunscreen

How to Prevent Skin Darkening

  • ✓ Apply sunscreen SPF 30 or higher
  • ✓ Wear sunglasses and hats
  • ✓ Avoid sun between 11 AM – 4 PM
  • ✓ Drink plenty of water
  • ✓ Use gentle face cleanser
  • ✓ Moisturize regularly

Dermatologist Recommended Tips

  • Reapply sunscreen every 2–3 hours
  • Use gel-based sunscreen for oily skin
  • Choose broad-spectrum sunscreen
  • Use Vitamin C serum in morning
  • Apply aloe vera for soothing

When to See a Dermatologist

  • Persistent tanning
  • Uneven skin tone
  • Dark patches on face
  • Skin irritation after sun exposure
  • Pigmentation that doesn’t fade

Early treatment can prevent long-term skin damage.

Conclusion

Skin darkening in summer is common but preventable. Proper sun protection, hydration, and skincare routine can help maintain healthy and glowing skin even during hot weather.

Protect your skin today to avoid pigmentation tomorrow.

title: How to Identify Your Skin Type at Home – Dermatologist Guide
heading: How to Identify Your Skin Type at Home – Simple Tests & Tips
content:
How to Identify Your Skin Type at Home – Dermatologist Guide

Understanding your skin type is the first step to building an effective skincare routine. Many people use products without knowing whether their skin is oily, dry, combination, sensitive, or normal. This often leads to breakouts, irritation, and dull skin. The good news is that you can easily identify your skin type at home using simple methods.

Why Knowing Your Skin Type is Important

Your skin type determines which cleanser, moisturizer, and sunscreen will work best. Using the wrong products can cause acne, dryness, excess oil production, and damage to your skin barrier. Once you understand your skin type, you can choose products that keep your skin healthy and balanced.

The Five Main Skin Types

  • Oily Skin: Shiny appearance, enlarged pores, frequent acne.
  • Dry Skin: Tight feeling, flakiness, rough texture.
  • Combination Skin: Oily T-zone with dry cheeks.
  • Normal Skin: Balanced oil and hydration.
  • Sensitive Skin: Easily irritated, redness, burning sensation.

Method 1: The Bare Face Test

This is one of the easiest and most reliable methods to identify your skin type.

  1. Wash your face with a gentle cleanser.
  2. Pat dry using a clean towel.
  3. Do not apply any skincare products.
  4. Wait for 30 minutes.
  5. Observe your skin in natural light.

If your face appears shiny all over, you likely have oily skin. If your skin feels tight or flaky, it is dry. If only the T-zone looks oily, you have combination skin. If your skin feels comfortable and balanced, it is normal. If redness or irritation appears, you may have sensitive skin.

Method 2: The Blotting Paper Test

This test helps measure oil production on different areas of your face.

  1. Take blotting paper or tissue.
  2. Press it gently on your forehead, nose, cheeks, and chin.
  3. Check how much oil is absorbed.

Excess oil from all areas indicates oily skin. Little to no oil suggests dry skin. Oil only in the T-zone means combination skin. Minimal oil overall usually indicates normal skin.

Method 3: The Touch Test

After cleansing your face and waiting for about an hour, gently touch your skin. Sticky skin suggests oily skin, tight skin indicates dryness, and balanced softness indicates normal skin.

How Weather Affects Your Skin Type

Skin type can change depending on the season. During summer, oil production increases, making skin appear more oily. In winter, dryness and flakiness become more common. Humid weather may lead to combination skin. It is important to adjust your skincare routine based on seasonal changes.

Common Mistakes People Make

  • Assuming acne means oily skin
  • Overwashing the face
  • Using harsh cleansers
  • Skipping moisturizer
  • Copying someone else's skincare routine

What to Do After Identifying Your Skin Type

  • Oily Skin: Use gel-based cleansers and lightweight moisturizers.
  • Dry Skin: Use hydrating creams and avoid harsh exfoliation.
  • Combination Skin: Balance hydration and target the T-zone.
  • Sensitive Skin: Choose fragrance-free gentle products.
  • Normal Skin: Maintain a simple routine and use sunscreen daily.

When to See a Dermatologist

  • Severe acne
  • Persistent dryness or oiliness
  • Frequent irritation
  • Uneven skin tone
  • Skin reactions to products

A dermatologist can perform detailed skin analysis and recommend personalized treatments.

Conclusion

Identifying your skin type at home is simple and helps you choose the right skincare products. By using the bare face test, blotting paper test, and observing your skin, you can determine your skin type easily. Once you understand your skin, you can build a routine that keeps it healthy and glowing.

Healthy skin starts with knowing your skin type.

title: Why Do Periods Become Irregular? Causes & Treatment
heading: Why Do Periods Become Irregular? – Common Causes & Effective Treatments
content:
Why Do Periods Become Irregular? – Complete Guide to Causes & Treatment

Irregular periods are one of the most common menstrual concerns affecting women of all ages. A normal menstrual cycle ranges from 21 to 35 days, with bleeding lasting 2 to 7 days. When your cycle becomes shorter, longer, unpredictable, or skips months, it is considered irregular. Understanding the root causes can help you take the right steps toward treatment and better reproductive health.

What Are Irregular Periods?

Irregular periods refer to any significant change in your normal menstrual pattern. This includes cycles shorter than 21 days, longer than 35 days, missed periods, extremely heavy or light bleeding, or bleeding between periods. Occasional irregularities are common, but persistent changes should be evaluated by a healthcare provider.

Common Causes of Irregular Periods

  • Hormonal Imbalance: The most frequent cause. Estrogen and progesterone levels can be disrupted by stress, weight changes, or medical conditions.
  • Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): Small cysts on the ovaries lead to excess androgen hormones, causing missed or infrequent periods.
  • Thyroid Disorders: Both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism can interfere with menstrual regularity.
  • Stress: High cortisol levels can suppress the hormones that regulate ovulation and menstruation.
  • Perimenopause: Women in their 40s experience fluctuating hormones, leading to irregular cycles before menopause.
  • Extreme Weight Changes: Being underweight or overweight affects estrogen production and ovulation.
  • Excessive Exercise: Intense physical training can disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis.
  • Uterine Fibroids or Polyps: Benign growths in the uterus can cause heavy, prolonged, or irregular bleeding.
  • Medications: Birth control pills, emergency contraception, blood thinners, and some antidepressants may alter your cycle.
  • Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID): Infections can cause irregular bleeding and pain.

Signs You Should See a Doctor

  • Sudden change in cycle length after years of regularity
  • Bleeding between periods or after sex
  • Periods occurring more often than every 21 days or less often than every 35 days
  • No period for 3 months (or 6 months if previously irregular)
  • Extremely heavy bleeding (soaking a pad/tampon every hour)
  • Severe pelvic pain or cramping
  • Signs of anemia like fatigue or dizziness

How Are Irregular Periods Diagnosed?

A doctor will typically start with a medical history and physical exam. Common diagnostic tests include:

  1. Blood tests to check hormone levels (FSH, LH, estrogen, progesterone, thyroid, prolactin)
  2. Pelvic ultrasound to look for fibroids, polyps, or PCOS
  3. Endometrial biopsy if needed
  4. Hysteroscopy for a closer view of the uterine cavity

Treatment Options for Irregular Periods

Treatment depends on the underlying cause. Here are common approaches:

  • Lifestyle Modifications: Stress reduction, balanced nutrition, maintaining a healthy weight, and moderate exercise can restore regular cycles.
  • Hormonal Birth Control: Pills, patches, rings, or IUDs can regulate cycles and reduce heavy bleeding.
  • Metformin: Often used for PCOS to improve insulin sensitivity and promote ovulation.
  • Thyroid Medication: Levothyroxine or anti-thyroid drugs for thyroid disorders.
  • Progesterone Therapy: Helps induce periods in women with low progesterone.
  • Surgery: Removal of fibroids, polyps, or ovarian cysts when necessary.
  • Fertility Treatments: Clomiphene or letrozole for women trying to conceive.

Home Remedies & Natural Approaches

  • Practice yoga and meditation to lower stress
  • Eat a fiber-rich diet with whole grains, vegetables, and lean protein
  • Include healthy fats like avocados and nuts to support hormone production
  • Avoid extreme dieting or excessive caffeine
  • Maintain a consistent sleep schedule
  • Track your cycle using an app or calendar

When Irregular Periods Are Normal

Certain life stages can cause temporary irregularities:

  • First 2 years after menarche: Teenage cycles are often irregular as the body matures.
  • After pregnancy or breastfeeding: It may take several months for cycles to return to normal.
  • Perimenopause: Irregular cycles are expected in women aged 40–50.
  • After stopping birth control: Can take 3–6 months to regulate.

Prevention Tips

While not all causes are preventable, you can reduce your risk by:

  • Managing stress effectively
  • Eating a well-balanced diet
  • Exercising moderately (avoid overtraining)
  • Getting enough sleep
  • Regular checkups with a gynecologist

Conclusion

Irregular periods can be distressing, but they are often treatable. Identifying the underlying cause through proper medical evaluation is the first step. Many women find relief through lifestyle changes, medication, or treating the primary condition. If you experience persistent irregularities, consult a healthcare provider to protect your long-term reproductive and overall health.

Remember – your menstrual cycle is a vital sign of your health.

title: Missed Period But Not Pregnant? 5 Possible Reasons
heading: Missed Period But Not Pregnant? – 5 Common Reasons & When to See a Doctor
content:
Missed Period But Not Pregnant? – 5 Possible Reasons Explained

A missed period often triggers an immediate thought: could I be pregnant? But when a pregnancy test comes back negative, many women feel confused and worried. The truth is, periods can be delayed or absent for many reasons other than pregnancy. Understanding these causes can help you identify what’s happening in your body and when to seek medical advice. For overall skin and hormonal health, you can always book a consultation with Dr. Anju Mehra.

What Is Considered a Missed Period?

A normal menstrual cycle ranges from 21 to 35 days. If you are more than 7 days late and have had a negative pregnancy test, it is considered a delayed period. When you miss three or more consecutive periods without being pregnant, it is called amenorrhea. Occasional delays are common, but persistent absence needs evaluation.

5 Possible Reasons for a Missed Period (Besides Pregnancy)

  • 1. Chronic Stress: High stress levels trigger cortisol production, which can suppress the hypothalamus — the part of your brain that regulates menstrual hormones. Even short-term intense stress can delay ovulation or stop it entirely, leading to a late or missed period. Stress also affects your skin; learn how weather changes and stress can trigger skin allergies.
  • 2. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): PCOS is one of the most common hormonal disorders in women of reproductive age. It causes an imbalance of reproductive hormones, leading to irregular ovulation or anovulation (no egg release). Many women with PCOS experience fewer than 9 periods per year. PCOS is also closely linked to hormonal acne, which a dermatologist can help manage.
  • 3. Thyroid Disorders: Both an overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism) and underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) can interfere with your menstrual cycle. The thyroid gland helps regulate metabolism and hormone production, and any imbalance can cause periods to become light, irregular, or stop altogether. If you’re experiencing unexplained hair fall along with period changes, read our guide on hair fall causes and treatments.
  • 4. Significant Weight Changes or Eating Disorders: Losing or gaining a large amount of weight rapidly can disrupt estrogen production. Being underweight (common in anorexia or extreme exercise) stops the body from producing enough estrogen. On the other hand, obesity can lead to excess estrogen, throwing off the normal cycle. Maintaining a healthy weight is also key for how diet affects your skin health.
  • 5. Perimenopause: Women in their 40s often enter perimenopause — the transition phase before menopause. During this time, estrogen and progesterone levels fluctuate unpredictably, causing skipped periods, shorter cycles, or longer gaps between periods. It is a natural part of aging, not a disease.

Other Possible Causes of a Missed Period (Beyond the Top 5)

  • Excessive Exercise: Female athlete triad syndrome (low energy, menstrual dysfunction, low bone density) is common in endurance athletes and dancers.
  • Medications: Birth control pills, hormonal IUDs, emergency contraception, antidepressants, and antipsychotics can alter or stop your period.
  • Chronic Diseases: Uncontrolled diabetes, celiac disease, and Cushing’s syndrome may affect menstrual regularity.
  • Primary Ovarian Insufficiency (POI): When ovaries stop functioning normally before age 40, periods may become irregular or stop.
  • Prolactin Imbalance (Hyperprolactinemia): High levels of prolactin (the milk-producing hormone) can suppress ovulation.

When Should You Take a Pregnancy Test Again?

Home pregnancy tests are very accurate when used correctly, but testing too early can give a false negative. Wait at least one week after your missed period. For best results, test first thing in the morning. If your period is 2 weeks late and the test is still negative, it is very unlikely you are pregnant — but a blood test at your doctor’s office can confirm.

Signs You Should See a Doctor

  • You have missed three or more periods in a row (and not pregnant)
  • You have missed one period but have symptoms like severe pelvic pain, unusual discharge, or fever
  • You are under 16 or over 45 with sudden cycle changes
  • You have signs of thyroid issues (fatigue, weight changes, hair loss, temperature sensitivity)
  • You have milky nipple discharge (possible high prolactin)
  • You are trying to conceive and cycles are irregular

How Is the Cause Diagnosed?

A gynecologist will typically perform:

  1. Detailed medical and lifestyle history (stress, exercise, diet, medications)
  2. Pelvic examination
  3. Blood tests: pregnancy test (beta-hCG), TSH (thyroid), prolactin, FSH, LH, estrogen, testosterone, and androgen levels
  4. Pelvic ultrasound to check ovaries, uterus, and endometrial thickness
  5. Sometimes a head MRI if prolactin is very high (to rule out pituitary tumor)

Treatment Options Based on the Cause

  • For stress-related missed periods: Therapy, relaxation techniques, yoga, adequate sleep, and time management often restore cycles within a few months.
  • For PCOS: Lifestyle changes (weight loss if overweight), metformin, hormonal birth control to regulate cycles, or ovulation induction if trying to conceive. For associated skin issues, check these acne treatment remedies.
  • For thyroid disorders: Levothyroxine (for hypothyroidism) or anti-thyroid drugs (for hyperthyroidism) normalize thyroid levels and usually restore menstruation.
  • For weight-related issues: Working with a dietitian to achieve a healthy BMI; treatment for eating disorders if present.
  • For perimenopause: Usually no treatment is needed unless symptoms are bothersome; low-dose birth control or hormone therapy can help regulate cycles.
  • For hyperprolactinemia: Dopamine agonist medications like cabergoline or bromocriptine.

Can You Induce a Missed Period Naturally?

While you cannot force a period to come, some lifestyle measures may help if the delay is due to stress or mild hormonal fluctuations:

  • Reduce intense exercise for a few weeks
  • Eat a balanced diet with healthy fats (avocado, nuts, olive oil)
  • Practice stress reduction (meditation, walking, deep breathing)
  • Maintain a regular sleep-wake cycle
  • Avoid crash dieting or rapid weight loss

Caution: Do not take herbal emmenagogues (like parsley tea or vitamin C in high doses) without medical advice — they can be ineffective or cause side effects.

When a Missed Period Is Normal

  • First 2 years after your first period (menarche): Cycles are often irregular as your body matures.
  • While breastfeeding: Prolactin suppresses ovulation, so periods may be absent for months.
  • After stopping hormonal birth control: It can take 3–6 months for natural cycles to resume.
  • During perimenopause (age 40–50): Skipping periods is expected.

Prevention Tips for Healthy Cycles

  • Manage chronic stress proactively
  • Maintain a stable, healthy weight
  • Avoid overtraining — include rest days in your fitness routine
  • Eat enough calories and nutrients (especially iron, vitamin D, and B vitamins)
  • Get your thyroid and prolactin levels checked if you have risk factors
  • Track your cycle using an app to notice patterns early

Conclusion

A missed period with a negative pregnancy test is usually not a cause for panic. The five most common reasons — stress, PCOS, thyroid issues, weight changes, and perimenopause — are all manageable with proper medical guidance. Occasional delays happen to many women. However, if you miss three or more consecutive periods, or if you have other concerning symptoms, schedule an appointment with a gynecologist or visit our clinic for expert advice. Your menstrual cycle is a valuable sign of your overall health — listen to what it’s telling you. For more skin, hair, and wellness tips, explore our complete blog library.
